Although India has maintained a neutral stance on the invasion,[469] reports indicate that artillery shells made by Indian manufacturers were sold to European countries and then diverted to Ukraine. Indian officials have not intervened despite objections from Russia.[470]

The Russian government has threatened retaliation against countries supplying military aid to Ukraine, and said it meant NATO was waging a proxy war against Russia.[471] According to the Atlantic Council's Peter Dickinson, Russia's government has not followed through on its threats, despite most of its "red lines" being crossed.[472] President Putin said that if military aid stopped, Ukraine would not survive for long.[473]

Since the start of Russia's invasion, the US has been sharing intelligence with Ukraine, with a focus on finished signal intelligence to aid in targeting.[474][475] In March 2025, the US paused intelligence sharing with Ukraine,[476] only to resume it a few days later.[477][478] In October 2025, the Wall Street Journal and Reuters reported that the US was to provide intelligence to enable long-range strikes on Russian energy infrastructure deep in Russian territory.[479][480][481] In November 2025, Reuters reported that the US threatened to cut intelligence sharing to pressure Ukraine into negotiating a peace agreement.[482]

By January 2026, according to French president Macron, the coalition of the willing were providing all of the international military support to Ukraine, with France providing two-thirds of the military intelligence.[483][484]

In April 2026, reports emerged that Ukrainian F-16 pilots have gone through an RAF-led program in the UK and were being instructed to operate in GPS-denied environments due to extensive Russian electronic warfare.[485][486]

Belarus has allowed Russia to use its territory to stage part of the invasion, and to launch missiles into Ukraine.[487] Belarus airspace was used by Russia, including for radar early warning and control missions, until 2023, when a Russian Beriev A-50 surveillance plane was damaged by drones.[488] Belarus is considered a co-belligerent.[489][490][491] Political scientist Matthew Frear considers Belarus a co-combatant, with "Lukashenko repeatedly stated his support for Putin's military actions",[492] while the 2023 issue Armed Conflict Survey classified it as not a direct co-combatant.[493] Belarus provided Russia with weapons and ammunition, and later, according to the 2024 issue of Armed Conflict Survey, Russia deployed tactical nuclear weapons in Belarus.[494]

In June 2023, US military intelligence suggested Iran was providing both Shahed combat drones and production materials to develop a drone manufactory to Russia.[495] In February 2024, a Reuters report indicated that Iran sent ballistic missiles to the Russian military.[496] According to the US and Ukraine, Iranian troops have been stationed in Crimea to assist Russia in launching drone attacks against civilians and civilian infrastructure.[497][498] Iran has denied sending arms to Russia for use against Ukraine.[399]

North Korea has supplied Russia with ballistic missiles and launchers.[499][500] In October 2024, Ukraine and South Korea claimed North Korean engineers had been deployed to the battlefield to help with the launch of these missiles, and suffered casualties.[501][502][503] In October 2024, Zelensky announced that Ukrainian intelligence believed there were 10,000 North Korean troops preparing to join Russian forces.[504] The North Korean government stated that none of their soldiers were fighting for Russia.[505][506][507]

US officials reported that North Korea had sent 3,000 soldiers to Russia for possible deployment to Ukraine, who had been transported by ship in October and were training at three military bases in eastern Russia.[508] On 28 October, NATO chief Mark Rutte confirmed earlier Ukrainian intelligence that North Korean troops had been deployed to Kursk Oblast to support Russia against the Kursk offensive, and the Pentagon reported that around 10,000 North Korean soldiers been sent in total.[509][510][511]

On 7 November, Ukraine's defence minister reported that North Korean troops had engaged in battle on 5 November in Kursk oblast,[512] a report that was confirmed by US and South Korean officials.[513][514][515][516] On 24 November, the Ukrainian chief of general staff confirmed that North Korean troops had been engaged in battle.[517] On 2 December the Pentagon noted that North Korean soldiers had been integrated into Russian units,[518] reports that were echoed by the Ukrainian intelligence directorate.[519] On 16 December, the US confirmed that North Korean troops had been killed in combat in Russia's Kursk oblast.[520] By 18 December, the number of killed and wounded North Korean soldiers had reportedly reached a couple of hundred,[521] while South Korea reported 100 North Koreans dead and 1000 injured.[522] US Army JAG officers Steve Szymanski and Joshua Keruski stated that North Korea had become a party to an international armed conflict with Ukraine as of the 5 November engagement.[523]

Zelenskyy showcased footage which he said showed Russian troops burning the faces of killed North Korean soldiers to conceal their presence on the battlefield.[524][525][526][527]

result.title: Page 12
result.summary: This text details Russia's military and economic efforts during the ongoing conflict, highlighting the integration of North Korean troops and laborers, the procurement of military technology from China and other nations, and the use of global trade networks to circumvent sanctions. It also addresses the continued flow of Russian fossil fuels and commodities to Western markets and concludes with an analysis of the difficulties in accurately tracking military and civilian casualties, noting significant loss of life in areas like Mariupol.
result.text:
In January 2025, two North Korean servicemen were taken prisoner while fighting in Kursk Oblast. Intelligence debriefings indicate that North Korean soldiers have been issued false military papers stating that they are Russians from Tuva.[528] In March 2025, South Korea's Joint Chiefs of Staff said that North Korea had sent an additional 3,000 soldiers early in the year, along with military equipment including short-range ballistic missiles. According to South Korea, roughly 11,000 North Korean soldiers had been sent to Russia, 4,000 of which were killed or wounded.[529] The same month, Russia acknowledged the presence of North Korean soldiers fighting in Kursk for the first time.[530]

In June 2025, former Russian defence minister Sergei Shoigu announced that 1,000 North Korean military workers would be deployed to Kursk for demining, and another 5,000 for reconstruction.[531] The BBC reported that, in 2024, more than 10,000 North Korean workers were sent to work in "slave-like conditions" in Russian construction and other sectors, "in violation of the UN sanctions banning the use of North Korean labour." A South Korean intelligence official told the BBC that as many as 50,000 workers were to be deployed in 2025.[532]

Politico reported in March 2023 that Chinese state-owned weapons manufacturer Norinco shipped assault rifles, drone parts, and body armour to Russia between June and December 2022, with some shipments via third countries including Turkey and the United Arab Emirates.[534] According to the US, Chinese ammunition has been used on battlefields in Ukraine.[535] In May 2023, the European Union identified that Chinese and UAE firms were supplying weapon components to Russia.[536] In April 2024, China was reported to have provided Russia with geospatial intelligence, machine tools for tanks, and propellants for missiles.[537] In September 2024, Reuters reported documents indicating Russia had established a weapons programme in China to develop and produce long-range attack drones, with assistance from local specialists, for use in the invasion.[538] In July 2025, Ukraine's Main Directorate of Intelligence reported that Laos had sent a 50-person demining crew to Kursk, though Laos denied the claim.[539]

Russia imports sensitive electronics, machinery, auto parts, and defence equipment from India.[540] Trade like oil sales has surged since 2022, boosting revenue for Russian state-owned companies. To bypass sanctions and manage its currency surplus, Russia pays in rupees, supporting both civilian and military needs.[541][542] Reuters reported in July 2025 that according to Indian customs data, an Indian company shipped military-use explosive compounds valued at $1.4 million to Russia in December 2024.[543]

Russia has continued to make billions from fossil fuel exports to the West. Ukraine's Western allies[which?] have paid Russia more for its hydrocarbons than they have given Ukraine in aid. In 2025 Slovakia and Hungary rejected an EU plan to phase out deliveries of Russian gas via Turkey by 2028. "Laundromat refineries" in Turkey and India process Russian crude and sell the refined fuel to sanctioning countries.[544][545][546] Russia has also developed partnerships with India and UAE that actively support its efforts to evade sanctions.[547][548][549]

In 2022, approximately 400,000 US-made semiconductors worth $53.6 million were shipped to Russia via Maldives, accounting for almost 20% of Maldives's exports. Maldives has no native semiconductor manufacturers; all of its exports are by Russian shell companies, most headquartered in Hong Kong.[550][551] From 2015 to 2021, average annual trade between Russia and Turkey in 45 military-linked materials was $28 million; from January to October 2023 it was $158 million.[552]

From January 2022 to mid-2025, the United States imported $24.51 billion of Russian goods, mainly fertilisers, enriched uranium and plutonium, and palladium.[553]

Russian and Ukrainian sources have both been said to inflate opposing casualty numbers and downplay their own losses for morale.[554] Leaked US documents say "under-reporting of casualties within the [Russian] system highlights the military's 'continuing reluctance' to convey bad news up the chain of command."[555] Russian news outlets have largely stopped reporting the Russian death toll.[556] Russia and Ukraine have admitted suffering "significant"[557] and "considerable" losses, respectively.[558][559]

The numbers of civilian and military deaths have been impossible to determine precisely.[560] Agence France-Presse (AFP) reported that neither it nor independent conflict monitors could verify Russian and Ukrainian claims of enemy losses and suspected they were inflated.[561] In October 2022, the independent Russian media project iStories, citing sources close to the Kremlin, reported that more than 90,000 Russian soldiers had been killed, seriously wounded, or gone missing.[562] Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelensky stated in February 2026 that approximately 55,000 Ukrainian soldiers had been killed on the battlefield, noting that a significant number were also officially listed as missing in action.[563][564][565]

While combat deaths can be inferred from a variety of sources, including satellite imagery of military action, measuring civilian deaths is more difficult. In June 2022, the Ukrainian Minister of Defence told CNN that tens of thousands of Ukrainians had died, and he hoped the death toll was below 100,000.[566] By July 2024, about 20,000 Ukrainians had lost limbs.[567] In Mariupol, Ukrainian officials believe at least 25,000 have been killed,[568][569] and bodies were still being discovered in September 2022.[570] The mayor said over 10,000 and possibly as many as 20,000 civilians died in the siege of Mariupol and Russian forces had brought mobile cremation equipment when they entered the city.[571][572][573] An investigation by AP from the end of 2022 estimated up to 75,000 killed civilians in the Mariupol area alone.[574][575] AFP says "a key gap in casualty counts is the lack of information from Russian-occupied places like the port city of Mariupol, where tens of thousands of civilians are believed to have died".[576] There were at least 8,000 excess deaths in Mariupol between March 2022 and February 2023.[577] The Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR) believed the true civilian casualty numbers were significantly higher than it has been able to confirm.[578]

result.title: Page 13
result.summary: The text details the severe human and demographic toll of the Russian invasion of Ukraine, highlighting disproportionate casualties among Russian ethnic minorities and a deepening Russian demographic crisis. It documents extensive Russian war crimes, including systematic attacks on civilians, infrastructure, and the use of banned chemical weapons. Furthermore, it covers the systematic human rights abuses in occupied territories—such as forced deportations, indoctrination, and coerced citizenship—and provides evidence of the torture, sexual violence, and extrajudicial execution of Ukrainian prisoners of war.
result.text:
In the Russian military, ethnic minorities have suffered disproportionately high casualties. In October 2022, the Russian regions with the highest death tolls were Dagestan, Tuva and Buryatia, all minority regions. In February 2024, six out of ten Russian regions with the highest mortality rates in Ukraine were located in Siberia and the far east, and ethnic minorities' continuing outsized casualty rates prompted analysts to warn that the situation will lead to long-term destructive impacts on these communities.[579][580][581][582][583] About 1,200 Russian soldiers were killed or wounded in Ukraine every day in May–June 2024,[584] which climbed to 1,500 by November 2024, when 45,690 casualties were estimated.[585] Newsweek estimated that on the bloodiest day in November 2024 the ratio of killed Russian men (1,950) exceeded the average daily male birth rate in Russia (1,836).[586] Latvia-based news outlet Meduza estimated that up to 140,000 Russian soldiers had died by July 2024.[587] In December 2025, an analysis by the BBC suggested that Russian casualties were growing at a faster rate than at any point previously in the invasion, with 40% more obituaries of soldiers being published in Russia in 2025 compared to 2024.[588][589] The war in Ukraine and the associated emigration, lower birth rates and war-related casualties further deepened the demographic crisis of Russia.[590]

The Russian invasion became the deadliest European war since World War II (1939-1945).[591] In February 2025, Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft estimated 250,000 dead.[592] In September 2024, The Wall Street Journal reported that there were now one million Ukrainians and Russians who were killed or wounded.[593]

The Russian military and authorities have been responsible for deliberate attacks against civilian targets[620] (including strikes on hospitals and on the energy grid), massacres of civilians, abduction and torture of civilians, sexual violence,[621] forced deportation of civilians, and torture and murder of Ukrainian prisoners of war. They have also carried out indiscriminate attacks in densely populated areas, including with cluster bombs, in one instance killing 61 people in the Kramatorsk railway station attack.[622][623][624][625] According to Kyrylo Budanov, the chief of the Ukrainian intelligence, Russia before the start of the invasion had created 'execution lists' of Ukrainian teachers, journalists, scientists, writers, priests, and politicians, and was preparing for a genocide of Ukrainians; the plans included locations of mass graves and mobile crematoria.[626]

According to the United Nations' Human Rights Office (OHCHR), by December 2023, about 78% of confirmed civilian casualties had been killed in Ukrainian-held territory.[628] Over 12,300 civilians have been killed since the invasion began.[629] Russia has deliberately and repeatedly attacked Ukrainian civilians with FPV drones, including first responders,[630] such as in the Kherson terror campaign dubbed the "human safari" [631][632][633] In October 2025, the UN concluded that the Russian military were systematically attacking Ukrainian civilians and civilian targets with drones along a 300-kilometre stretch of the Dnipro River, to drive Ukrainians out of the region. The report said that these are war crimes and crimes against humanity.[634]

The UN Human Rights Office reports that Russia is committing severe human rights violations in occupied Ukraine, including arbitrary detentions, enforced disappearances, torture, crackdown on protests and freedom of speech, enforced Russification, indoctrination of children, and suppression of Ukrainian language and culture.[635][636] The UN also found that Russian authorities were systematically deporting Ukrainian civilians from occupied provinces, which is a crime against humanity.[634] Ukrainians have been coerced into taking Russian passports and becoming Russian citizens. Those who refuse are denied healthcare and other rights,[636] and can be imprisoned as a "foreign citizen". Ukrainian men who take Russian citizenship are drafted to fight against Ukraine.[637]

Russian forces have reportedly used banned chemical weapons, usually tear gas grenades.[638][639] In April 2024, a Daily Telegraph investigation concluded that "Russian troops are carrying out a systematic campaign of illegal chemical attacks against Ukrainian soldiers".[640]

In January 2026, the UN reported that 2025 had been the deadliest year for Ukrainian civilians since 2022, with 2,514 deaths and 12,142 injuries being verified during the year, a 31% increase compared to 2024. The report said that a "massive increase" of Russian long-range weapons was causing increased harm in urban centres.[641][642]

An August 2022 report by the Humanitarian Research Lab of the Yale School of Public Health identified 21 filtration camps for Ukrainian "civilians, POWs [prisoners of war], and other personnel" in the vicinity of Donetsk oblast. Imaging of one camp, Olenivka prison, found two sites of disturbed earth consistent with "potential graves".[643] Kaveh Khoshnood, a professor at the Yale School of Public Health, said: "Incommunicado detention of civilians is more than a violation of international humanitarian law--it represents a threat to the public health of those currently in the custody of Russia and its proxies." Conditions described by freed prisoners include exposure, insufficient access to sanitation, food and water, cramped conditions, electrical shocks and assault.[643]

An OHCHR report released in November 2022 documented abuses on both sides, based on interviews with prisoners.[644] In March 2023, UN human rights commissioner Volker Türk reported that more than 90% of the Ukrainian POWs interviewed by his office said they had been tortured or ill-treated in penitentiary facilities, which Russia despite several requests did not give UN staff access to.[645]

In April 2023, several videos circulated on different websites purportedly showing Russian soldiers beheading Ukrainian soldiers.[646]

In March 2024, the UN issued a report saying Russia may have executed more than 30 recently captured Ukrainian POWs over the winter. The UN Human Rights Office verified three incidents in which Russian servicemen executed seven Ukrainian servicemen. According to the same report, 39 of 60 released Ukrainian POWs said that they had been subjected to sexual violence during their internment.[647]

result.title: Page 14
result.summary: The text details widespread allegations of Russian war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide in Ukraine, including the systematic torture and execution of prisoners, the forced deportation and assimilation of Ukrainian children, and the deliberate destruction of civilian infrastructure, healthcare systems, and cultural heritage. It covers international legal responses, including ICC arrest warrants for Russian leadership and the formation of a special tribunal for the crime of aggression. Additionally, it highlights the severe humanitarian consequences of the invasion, such as mass displacement, food insecurity, and the documentation of these atrocities by international bodies and investigative organizations.
result.text:
In October 2024, the EEAS described increasing frequency of Russian executions of Ukrainian prisoners, with at least 177 prisoners dying in Russian captivity since the beginning of the war. The statement included the OHCHR's confirmation of systematic use of different methods of torture used by Russians against Ukrainian prisoners.[648]

The Ukrainian Armed Forces have been accused of executions and abuses of Russian POWs, but the number of allegations has been significantly lower.[649]: para. 105[650][651][652]

In June 2024, an investigation by the Financial Times identified four Ukrainian children on a Russian government-linked adoption website who had been abducted from state care homes. The children's Ukrainian background was not mentioned. One child was shown with a new Russian name and an age that differed from their Ukrainian documents, while another was shown using a Russian version of their Ukrainian name. 17 other matches identified by the Financial Times on the adoption website were confirmed as Ukrainian children in a New York Times investigation. Ukrainian authorities estimate that nearly 20,000 Ukrainian children have been forcibly taken from occupied territories to Russia since the full-scale invasion began. Wayne Jordash, president of humanitarian law firm Global Rights Compliance, described forcibly transferring or deporting children as war crimes, adding that when done as part of a widespread or systematic attack on a civilian population, Russia is also committing crimes against humanity.[653][654]

A March 2025 report published by the ISW suggests that the mass transfer of Ukrainian children to Russia had been an established component of Russia's war plans in Ukraine prior to the invasion. It references Russian governmental documents that predate the invasion which outlined plans to move Ukrainian orphans in occupied Ukraine to Russia, described as 'humanitarian evacuations'. Since the invasion, tens to hundreds of thousands of Ukrainian children have been deported to Russia, where they have been housed in camps, placed with adoptive families, and provided with new Russian identities including new or altered names, falsified birth certificates, and other state documentation. The report claims that Russia intends to convert the abductees into 'the next generation of Russians' whilst 'depriving [Ukraine] of its multi-generation potential'. Referencing Article 2 of the Genocide Convention, the author concludes the opening section by noting that 'International law explicitly forbids the forcible transfer of children from one group to another group for the purpose of destroying, in whole or in part, a national or ethnic group, and considers these violations as constituent acts of genocide.'[655]

In August 2025, Mykola Kuleba, the head of a Ukrainian NGO, said that Russian occupation authorities in Ukraine had created an online "catalog" of Ukrainian children up for adoption sorted by physical traits, denouncing the practice as child trafficking.[656][657][658][659]

The International Criminal Court (ICC) opened an investigation into possible crimes against humanity, genocide and war crimes committed in Ukraine.[660] On 17 March 2023, the ICC issued a warrant for Putin's arrest, charging him with individual criminal responsibility in the abduction of children forcibly deported to Russia.[661] It was the first time that the ICC had issued an arrest warrant for the head of state of a permanent member of the United Nations Security Council[661] (the world's five principal nuclear powers).[662] Moscow has denied any involvement in war crimes, a response Vittorio Bufacchi of University College Cork says "has bordered on the farcical",[663] and its contention that the images coming out of Bucha were fabricated "a disingenuous response born by delusional hubris, post-truth on overdrive, (that) does not merit to be taken seriously." Even the usually fractured United States Senate came together to call Putin a war criminal.[664]

One of several efforts to document Russian war crimes concerns its repeated bombardment of markets and bread lines, destruction of basic infrastructure and attacks on exports and supply convoys, in a country where deliberate starvation of Ukrainians by Soviets still looms large in public memory.[665] Forcible deportation of populations, such as took place in Mariupol, is another area of focus.[666]

In March 2024, the ICC issued arrest warrants for military officials Sergey Kobylash, Viktor Sokolov, Sergei Shoigu and Valery Gerasimov.[667][668] In May 2026, a special tribunal was formalized by 36 countries to prosecute Putin for the crime of aggression against Ukraine.[669]

The invasion contributed to the 2022 food crises.[670] As of February 2025, 3.7 million Ukrainians were internally displaced and 6.9 million were refugees.[671]

Russian attacks on civilians, causing mass civilian casualties and displacement, have been characterised as genocide and democide.[25][26][27][28] In September 2023, a UN-mandated investigative body reported that Russian occupiers had tortured Ukrainians to death, and forced families to listen as they raped women.[672] The commission previously found that violations committed by Russian forces in Ukraine may constitute crimes against humanity.[673]

By August 2024, the WHO had recorded 1,940 attacks against Ukrainian healthcare and reported widespread double-tap attacks.[674] In 2023, Physicians for Human Rights described Russian attacks on Ukraine's healthcare system as having a "reasonable basis" to be considered war crimes, and could potentially constitute crimes against humanity.[675]

As of August 2025, UNESCO has verified damage to 508 Ukrainian cultural sites, including 151 religious sites, 34 museums, 33 monuments, and 18 libraries.[676] In 2022, the European Parliament labelled Russia's destruction of Ukrainian cultural property a war crime.[677] Ukraine's Minister of Culture Oleksandr Tkachenko called it cultural genocide.[678]

A documentary film produced during the siege of Mariupol, 20 Days in Mariupol, won the Oscar for best documentary in 2024.[679]

result.title: Page 15
result.summary: The war in Ukraine has triggered a massive humanitarian crisis, resulting in millions of refugees and internally displaced persons, while Russia faces accusations of forced deportations. The conflict has caused severe environmental destruction—termed ecocide—including industrial pollution, forest loss, and damage to the Kakhovka Dam, alongside global climate impacts. Furthermore, the war has created nuclear security risks at occupied power plants, involved nuclear rhetoric from Russia, and caused a catastrophic decline in Ukraine's GDP.
result.text:
The war has caused the largest refugee and humanitarian crisis in Europe since World War II.[680] In the first week of the invasion, the UN reported over a million refugees had fled Ukraine; this reached over eight million by February 2023.[681][682] In May 2022, following an influx of military equipment into Ukraine, a significant number of refugees sought to return to regions relatively isolated from the front in southeastern Ukraine.[683] By 3 May, another 8 million people were displaced inside Ukraine.[684]

Most refugees were women, children, elderly, or disabled.[685] Most male Ukrainian nationals aged 18 to 60 were denied exit from Ukraine as part of mandatory conscription,[686] unless they were responsible for the financial support of three or more children, single fathers, or were the parent/guardian of children with disabilities.[687] Many Ukrainian men, including teenagers, opted to remain in Ukraine voluntarily to join the resistance.[688]

According to the UN High Commission for Refugees as of May 2022, there were 3,315,711 refugees in Poland, 901,696 in Romania, 594,664 in Hungary, 461,742 in Moldova, 415,402 in Slovakia, and 27,308 in Belarus, while Russia reported it had received over 800,104 refugees.[681] By July 2022, over 390,000 Ukrainian refugees had arrived in the Czech Republic.[689] Turkey registered more than 58,000 Ukrainian refugees as of March 2022.[690] The EU invoked the Temporary Protection Directive for the first time, granting Ukrainian refugees the right to live and work in the EU for up to three years.[691] Britain has accepted 146,379 refugees, as well as extending the ability to remain in the UK for three years.[692] Flight from war affected especially the southern and eastern regions and especially educated women of child-bearing age and their children. With an estimate of more than 20% of refugees not returning, study author Maryna Tverdostup concluded that long-term shrinking will significantly impair reconstruction.[693]

According to the Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe (OSCE), Russia has engaged in "massive deportation" of over 1.3 million Ukrainian civilians, potentially constituting crimes against humanity.[694] The OSCE and Ukraine have accused Russia of forcibly moving civilians to filtration camps in Russian-held territory, and then into Russia. Ukrainian sources have compared this policy to Soviet-era population transfers and Russian actions in the Chechen War of Independence.[695] For instance, as of April 2022, Russia claimed to have evacuated about 121,000 Mariupol residents to Russia.[695][696] RIA Novosti and Ukrainian officials said that thousands were dispatched to centres in cities in Russia and Russian-occupied Ukraine,[697] from which people were sent to economically depressed regions of Russia.[698][699][l]

Based on a preliminary assessment, the war has inflicted $51 billion in environmental damage in Ukraine; according to a report by the Yale School of the Environment, 623,000 tonnes (1.4 billion pounds) of petrochemicals have burned as a result of shelling, while nearly 1,500 tonnes (3.2 million pounds) of pollutants have leaked into bodies of water. Hazardous chemicals have contaminated around 28 hectares (70 acres) of soil, and likely made agricultural activities temporarily impossible.[701] Around 30% of Ukraine's land is littered with explosives and more than 2.4 million hectares (5.9 million acres) of forest have been damaged.[702]

According to peace organisation PAX, Russia's "deliberate targeting of industrial and energy infrastructure" has caused "severe" pollution, and the use of explosive weapons has left "millions of tonnes" of contaminated debris in cities and towns.[703] In early June 2023, the Kakhovka Dam, under Russian occupation, was damaged, causing flooding and triggering warnings of an "ecological disaster".[704]

The Ukrainian government, international observers and journalists have described the damage as ecocide.[705] The Ukrainian government is investigating more crimes against the environment and ecocide (a crime in Ukraine).[706] Zelenskyy has met with prominent European figures (Heidi Hautala, Margot Wallstrom, Mary Robinson and Greta Thunberg) to discuss the environmental damage and how to prosecute it.[707]

According to an investigation by NGL Media published in April 2024, Russia has completely destroyed over 60,000 hectares (150,000 acres) of Ukrainian forests. The investigation stated that long-term ecological consequences may include lowering of the groundwater level, reduction of biodiversity, worsening of air quality, fire outbreaks, and rivers and ponds drying up.[708]

The war in Ukraine has severely disrupted global climate policy and increased CO2 emissions.[709][710] The effects have been strongly felt in Asia,[711][712][713] Europe,[714]

and the US.[715]

Putin implied that Russia may use nuclear weapons if certain "red lines" were crossed. By 2024, most of the Russian government's "red lines" had been crossed without nuclear weapons being used in response.[716]

The invasion had an impact on Ukraine's nuclear power plants. Russian forces captured Chernobyl on the first day, leading to a huge spike in radiation levels.[717] Russia also captured Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant, the largest in Europe, which has since been at risk and damaged by shelling. In August 2022, Zelenskyy described the situation as "Russian nuclear terror".[718] The International Atomic Energy Agency said it was the first time a military conflict occurred amid nuclear plants, and it called for a demilitarised zone around Zaporizhzhia NPP.[719]

Ukrainian Minister of Economic Development and Trade Yulia Svyrydenko announced that for 2022 Ukraine had a 30% loss in their gross domestic product (GDP).[720] The International Monetary Fund predicted that Ukraine's GDP would decrease between 10% to 35%;[721] the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development predicted a 20% decrease.[722] The

result.title: Page 16
result.summary: The text details the economic consequences of the Russia-Ukraine war, noting Ukraine's GDP growth and reconstruction needs alongside a severe humanitarian crisis, and Russia's mounting financial costs, reliance on China, and internal economic instability. It covers trade disputes over grain, the role of natural resources in the conflict, and international sanctions on Russian energy. Finally, it outlines the history of failed peace negotiations, the divergent territorial and security demands of both nations, the significant shift in US policy under the Trump administration toward pro-Russian peace proposals, and widespread international condemnation of the invasion.
result.text:
Ukrainian statistics service said that the GDP of Ukraine in 2023 grew by 5.3%.[723] By 2026 the total cost of rebuilding Ukraine after the war was estimated by the World Bank at US$588 billion.[724][725]

Ukraine began issuing war bonds on 1 March 2022, and the following day the Ukrainian government announced that they had raised 6.14 billion hryvnias.[726] In May 2022 the European Commission banned grain sales in Bulgaria, Poland, Hungary, Romania and Slovakia, with the only exception being if they were transiting through those countries; the ban was lifted in September 2023.[727]

The war has caused a major humanitarian crisis in Ukraine: the United Nations Development Programme calculated in March 2022 that a prolonged conflict would cause 30% of the Ukrainian population to fall below the poverty line, while a further 62% would be at risk of falling into poverty within a year.[728]

Ukraine is home to substantial mineral, fossil-fuel, and agricultural resources, the capture of which analysts believe was a potential motivation for the launching of the war by Russia.[729][730] Joint exploitation of Ukrainian mineral resources was subsequently the subject of the Ukraine–United States Mineral Resources Agreement.[731]

The US government estimates that Russia's economic losses from the war and Western sanctions will amount to around $1.3 trillion by 2025, and Russia's direct financial spending on the invasion is estimated at $250 billion (as of late 2024) – costs that Russia could not have foreseen.[732] Other estimates put the direct cost of the war to Russia at over US$450 billion.[732][733][734] The international sanctions led Russia to become heavily economically and technologically dependent on China.[735][736]

The Russian Ministry of Economic Development said that for 2022 the GDP contracted by 2.1%[737] and for 2023 Russia's government said the GDP grew by 3.6%.[738] In April 2024, it was reported that Russia was planning tax increases to help finance the war.[739] In January 2025, it was reported that, since early 2022, Russia had used a two-prong strategy to finance the war. In addition to the official Russian government defence budget—direct financial expenditure for the war was estimated at US$250 billion through June 2024 for military spending through normal channels,[732] with the military budget rising to over 20% of annual GDP—an additional off-budget financing mechanism was employed with over US$200 billion of debt funding obtained from preferential bank loans to defence contractors and war-related businesses, compelled by the Russian government.[733][734]

A report published in April 2025 by the Stockholm Institute of Transition Economics noted that official Russian statistics are unreliable.[740] The report argued that Russian fiscal stimulus has kept the economy afloat so far, but that it is on an unsustainable trajectory.[740][741] In August 2025, VEB, one of the largest Russian state banks, assessed that the Russian economy had started slipping into recession.[742] In April 2026, Putin said Russia's GDP shrank by combined 1.8% in January and February; according to Fortune, Russian officials also warned Putin earlier in the year about a financial crisis being possible due to "spiraling" inflation.[743]

A price cap was placed on Russian oil by the G7 in December 2022.[744] The US banned all imports of Russian oil in March 2022.[745] The EU placed an embargo on oil products from Russia in February 2023.[744] Other countries that embargoed Russian oil included Five Eyes partners Canada, the United Kingdom, Australia and New Zealand.[746]

Peace negotiations between Russia and Ukraine took place on 28 February, 3 March, and 7 March 2022 in Belarus. Further talks were held on 10 March and 14 March in Turkey.[747] Peace negotiations in Istanbul, Turkey, began on 29 March 2022 and ended without agreement.

Since 2024, Ukraine's main peace terms are that Russia withdraw its troops, that Ukrainian prisoners be released, Russian leaders be prosecuted for war crimes, and Ukraine be given security guarantees to prevent further aggression. Russia's main terms are that Russia must keep all the land it occupies, that it be given all of the provinces that it claims but does not fully control, that Ukraine end plans to join NATO, and that sanctions against Russia be lifted.[749] According to Western analysts, allowing Russia to keep the land it seized would "reward the aggressor while punishing the victim" and set a dangerous precedent.[750] They predicted this would encourage Russia "to continue its imperialist campaign of expansionism", and embolden other expansionist regimes.[750][751][752][753]

After Donald Trump became US president in 2025, there was a major shift in US policy. The Trump administration began negotiations with Russia and separately with Ukraine. In February 2025, the US twice sided with Russia in UN resolutions, opposing a European-drafted resolution condemning Russia's actions and supporting the territorial integrity of Ukraine, and then drafting and voting for a UN Security Council resolution calling for the end of the conflict, but containing no criticism of Russia.[754]

Since March 2025, Ukraine has accepted a proposal for a 30-day unconditional ceasefire, which is supported by the US and European allies.[755] Russia has rejected calls for a ceasefire.[756][757][758] In November 2025, Trump adopted a 28-point peace plan, interpreted as broadly pro-Russian,[759][760] and according to The Insider, at its core a recycled Russian document substantially written by Kirill Dmitriev, a Kremlin operative.[761] Ukraine, European allies and the US then produced revised versions. These were rejected by the Russians.[762]

The invasion received widespread international condemnation from governments and intergovernmental organisations.[763] In March 2022 and February 2023, 141 member states of the UN General Assembly voted for a resolution that Russia should

result.title: Page 17
result.summary: The text details the global response to the Russian invasion of Ukraine, covering the implementation of extensive economic sanctions, the provision of international military and humanitarian aid, and the resulting shifts in global energy and diplomatic relations. It highlights worldwide public reactions, including protests and cyberattacks, alongside Russia's domestic crackdown on dissent. Furthermore, it outlines the geopolitical divide in international support, the formal labeling of Russia as a state sponsor of terrorism by various nations, and the expansion of NATO through the membership of Finland and Sweden.
result.text:
immediately withdraw. Seven, including Russia, opposed.[764] Political reactions to the invasion included new sanctions on Russia, which triggered widespread effects on the Russian and world economies.[765] As of July 2025, the EU had adopted 18 packages of sanctions against Russia and Belarus.[766] Sanctions forced Russia to reorient its oil exports, rely more on LNG (which was not subject to EU sanctions), and shift its coal exports from Europe to Asia.[767] Most European countries cancelled nuclear cooperation with Russia.[768]

Over 70 countries and the EU delivered humanitarian aid to Ukraine, and nearly 50 countries plus the EU provided military aid.[769] Economic sanctions included bans on Russian aircraft using EU airspace,[770] certain Russian banks from the SWIFT payments system, and certain Russian media outlets.[771] Reactions to the invasion have included public and media responses, peace efforts, and the examination of the invasion's legality.

Demonstrations were held worldwide.[772] Calls for a boycott of Russian goods spread on social media,[773] while hackers attacked Russian websites.[774] Anti-Russian sentiment against Russians living abroad surged.[775] In March 2022, Russian president Putin introduced prison sentences of up to 15 years for publishing "fake news" about Russian military operations,[776] intended to suppress any criticism related to the war.[777]

Some countries, particularly in the Global South, saw public sympathy or outright support for Russia, due in part to distrust of US foreign policy.[778] According to the Economist Intelligence Unit in 2023, 31 percent of the world's population live in countries that are leaning towards or supportive of Russia, 30.7 percent live in neutral countries, and 36.2 percent live in countries that are against Russia in some way.[779]

A number of supranational and national parliaments passed resolutions declaring Russia to be a state sponsor of terrorism.[780] By October 2022, Latvia, Lithuania and Estonia had declared Russia a terrorist state.[781] On 1 August 2023, Iceland became the first European country to close its embassy in Russia as a result of the invasion.[782]

The invasion prompted Ukraine,[783] Finland and Sweden to apply for NATO membership.[784] Finland became a member in 2023,[785] and Sweden in 2024.[786]
